skill.name: memory-safety-patterns
skill.description: Apply memory-safe programming patterns for Rust, C++, and C, including RAII, ownership, smart pointers, bounds checking, and safe resource cleanup.
skill.summary: Guide systems code toward safer ownership, cleanup, bounds checking, and concurrency patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found command execution, filesystem, network, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review shows they are documentation examples inside markdown. No executable skill code, prompt injection, credential access, exfiltration behavior, or malicious intent was found. The skill is safe to publish with low residual risk from copied examples being used without local review.

low_findings.title: External Command Patterns Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged thread spawning, markdown code fences, resource references, and diagnostic shell commands. These are non-executable examples in SKILL.md and the implementation playbook, not skill automation or command invocation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are markdown instructions or fenced code samples. No script file or runtime path executes these commands.

low_findings.title: Reference Links Are Not Network Exfiltration
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to public C++, Rust, and AddressSanitizer documentation. They are static reference links and do not send local data to an external service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only as markdown links in the resources section. No fetch, curl, webhook, or request logic was found.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Write Pattern Is a C Cleanup Example
low_findings.description: The flagged file write is a C example that opens log.txt while demonstrating cleanup logic. It is not executed by the skill and does not alter files during normal skill use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The operation is inside a fenced C code sample. There is no wrapper script or instruction that automatically runs it.

low_findings.title: Blocker Labels Are Memory Safety Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: Static blocker findings for weak cryptography and reconnaissance map to memory-safety text, C symbols, and resource-management examples. No cryptographic implementation, system probing routine, or network reconnaissance behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found memory safety explanations and sample APIs at the flagged lines. The semantic context does not support malware or reconnaissance intent.

content.value_statement: Memory bugs can cause crashes, leaks, data races, and exploitable behavior in systems software. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code apply safer ownership, cleanup, and concurrency patterns across Rust, C++, and C.
actual_capabilities: Explains RAII, ownership, smart pointers, and cleanup patterns for systems code.
Compares Rust, C++, C, and garbage-collected approaches to resource safety.
Provides examples for preventing use-after-free, double-free, leaks, and bounds errors.
Suggests safer concurrency patterns using ownership, mutexes, atomics, and locks.
Lists debugging tools such as AddressSanitizer, Valgrind, Miri, and ThreadSanitizer.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not an automated static analyzer.
It cannot prove memory safety for a complete codebase without tests and tooling.
C and C++ examples may require adaptation for project standards and compilers.
It does not replace expert review for unsafe Rust or low-level C interfaces.

use_cases.title: Design Safer Systems Code
use_cases.description: Use ownership, RAII, and cleanup patterns before writing new low-level modules.

use_cases.title: Review Risky Resource Handling
use_cases.description: Identify safer alternatives for raw pointers, manual cleanup, shared ownership, and lock management.

use_cases.title: Teach Memory Safety Concepts
use_cases.description: Explain common memory bugs and prevention techniques with language-specific examples.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a Memory Bug
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a suspected memory issue.
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain this memory safety issue in plain language. Identify the bug category and one safer pattern to use.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Safe Ownership Model
prompt_templates.scenario: Select patterns before implementing a component.
prompt_templates.prompt: Recommend an ownership and cleanup model for this component. Compare Rust, C++, and C options with tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Manual Resource Management
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve existing code that opens files, sockets, or heap memory.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this resource management code. Suggest RAII, smart pointer, or cleanup-label changes that reduce leaks and double frees.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Concurrent Shared State
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of synchronization and data-race risk.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this concurrent code for data races, lifetime errors, and unsafe sharing. Recommend ownership, locking, or atomic changes with verification steps.

output_examples.input: A C module allocates memory, opens a file, and returns early on several error paths.
output: Classifies the main risk as leaks during early return paths.
Recommends a single cleanup path that releases resources in reverse acquisition order.
Suggests tests for allocation failure, file-open failure, and successful cleanup.

output_examples.input: A C++ class owns a socket and can be copied by default.
output: Identifies double-close risk from accidental copying.
Recommends RAII ownership, deleted copy operations, and explicit move behavior.
Suggests validating behavior with destructor and move tests.

output_examples.input: A Rust service shares mutable state between worker threads.
output: Checks whether ownership and synchronization are explicit.
Compares mutexes, read-write locks, atomics, and channels for the workload.
Recommends verification with compiler checks and concurrency tests.

best_practices: Start with ownership and lifetime boundaries before choosing specific data structures.
Prefer RAII, smart pointers, and safe containers over manual cleanup when available.
Pair design changes with sanitizer, leak, bounds, and concurrency tests.
anti_patterns: Do not copy examples into production without adapting error handling and project standards.
Do not use raw pointers for ownership when a safer abstraction is available.
Do not treat unsafe Rust or manual C cleanup as safe without focused review.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code?
faq.answer: No. It provides markdown guidance and examples for memory-safe programming patterns.

faq.question: Which languages does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Rust, C++, C, and general comparisons with garbage-collected languages.

faq.question: Can it replace AddressSanitizer or Valgrind?
faq.answer: No. It helps choose patterns, but runtime tools are still needed to verify behavior.

faq.question: Is it useful for Rust code?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains ownership, borrowing, shared ownership, unsafe blocks, and concurrency patterns.

faq.question: Is it useful for C code?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers cleanup labels, create and destroy APIs, and careful ownership documentation.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk low?
faq.answer: The flagged commands, URLs, and file operations are documentation examples, not executable skill behavior.
